mnemonics.title: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics
mnemonics.content: SNIPSUPAC: System status visible, NIce error messages, User control, System matches reality, Error prevention, Help users recognize/recover, Documentation, Visibility of system status (repeat), Aesthetic design, Shortcuts, Universal usability, Parental guidance (help)

mnemonics.title: Atomic Design Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: A-M-O-T-P: Atoms (buttons, inputs) → Molecules (search bar) → Organisms (header) → Templates (page layout) → Pages (final UI)

mnemonics.title: WCAG Accessibility Principles
mnemonics.content: POUR: Perceivable (information presented in ways users can perceive), Operable (interface components must be operable), Understandable (interface must be understandable), Robust (content must be robust)

mnemonics.title: Design System Components
mnemonics.content: STYLES: Style Guide, Tokens, Your components, Libraries, Examples, Standards

acronyms.usage: The structural organization of content in an app/website, including navigation and hierarchy
The visual and interactive elements through which users interact with a product
The overall experience of a user when interacting with a product
International standards for making web content accessible to people with disabilities
Style sheet language for describing the presentation of a document
Vector image format that scales without quality loss, ideal for UI icons

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a checkerboard where every square is 8x8 pixels. All buttons, inputs, margins use multiples of 8 (8, 16, 24, 32). Align elements to this invisible grid for consistency.
Use the 'squint test' - squint your eyes at the design. The elements that stand out most prominently should be the most important. Larger, bolder, higher contrast = higher importance.
Group related items together using proximity. Make similar items look similar (same color, shape). Use white space to separate distinct sections.
Wireframe = skeletal outline (blueprint). Prototype = working model (dollhouse). Wireframe shows WHERE things go, prototype shows HOW they work.

analogies.analogy: Design guidelines are like a recipe - they provide ingredients (components) and instructions (rules) to create consistent, delicious results every time.
A design system is like a LEGO set - standardized pieces (components) with instruction manuals (guidelines) that can build infinite structures (products).
A wireframe is like an architectural blueprint - shows where walls, doors, and windows go without the paint, furniture, or decorations.
Personas are like character profiles in a movie - they represent different audience types to help designers write the 'script' (UI) that works for everyone.
Responsive design is like water - it adapts its shape to fit any container (device) while maintaining its essential properties.
